ok, I think I understand our key difference:
In my view, AggregateFn is the core of aggregation where the aggregation logic is defined by `init, accumulate, merge, finalize`, there is no concept of `on` here since it's embedded inside `accumulate` lambda (like accumulate takes a row and it can do whatever to extract the on). So to me, `on` and predefined max, mean, etc are all syntax sugar that help users to create a list of AggregateFn.

In your view, `on` is also the core of aggregation. AggregateFn is just the algorithm and we can combine it with different `on` to create different `Aggregation` to do.

I think your way of abstraction is better: basically we decouple aggregation algorithm and the actual data we aggregate on. So AggregateFn is just the pure algorithm and Aggregate contains information about a particular instance of aggregation like `on` and `name`.

Well yeah, but only if you need to know that the value is actually `None`. However, all that is being checked here is if the value is set, otherwise it's being set to the default.

In this case, it is written as an anti-pattern. My comment here is implicitly recommends avoiding this pattern by using the more logical pattern of handling `None` and other "empty" objects:

```
if not value:
  do this
else:
  do that
```

Why would you check for "false" first if you can just do the following which is less confusing to read (more important for complicated conditionals):

```
if value:
  do that
else:
  do this
```

`None` is falsy meaning you can safely use `if [str, None]` and it will never be true for `None`. The other advantage of not checking `None` here is that it will also catch empty objects like `""`, `[]`, `()`, `{}`, etc. and fallback to the default behavior. As it is written, those objects will require the user to read through a code traceback when it inevitably fails because the user used the wrong empty type. I think it's important here because there's no type hint, so I'm not actually sure what `cdslib` is supposed to be. 

Regarding the PEPs warning:

>Also, beware of writing if x when you really mean if x is not None – e.g. when testing whether a variable or argument >that defaults to None was set to some other value. The other value might have a type (such as a container) that could >be false in a boolean context!

It doesn't recommend one way or another. However, I use type-hints for this reason so the user knows what is expected and what isn't. In that case, it is safe to make assumptions about how the data can be handled as the interfaces requirements are explicit.

There are multiple issues for this (#3815, #2900). I looked into it.

Expectation: The docstring of static property `FooClass.bar_prop` is shown in `help(FooClass)` and `help(FooClass.bar_prop)`.

Problem:
Both `help` calls read the documentation from `FooClass.bar_prop.__doc__`. However, `FooClass.bar_prop` is already the value of the property, so `__doc__` refers to the class of the value, not to the property.
So fundamentally this is as impossible as adding docstrings to attributes. (See rejected PEP 224: https://legacy.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0224/ )

Possible work-around:
It could be done like for enums. List the static properties in the documentation of the parent class:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pybind11.h#L1961

Hi Cliff, The `(1+cos(tilt))` in the denominator refers to Eq. 11 in the paper, because the diffuse POA component already contains the simple single-row view factor `(1+cos(tilt))/2` so I call this `f_sky_pv_shade` an "adjustment factor":

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1385621/142360821-eb8f110c-3921-4d42-b54e-01850ecb7f7b.png)

Also, importantly, we found that this "averaging" approach only worked for fixed tilt, and is a **BAD** assumption when the rack tilt approaches horizontal, as it would for trackers at noon, when the _linear_ assumption falls apart. So ignore Eq. 15, 16, 17, & 18. Do I need to publish an _errata_ in the proceedings?

Also more importantly, it's completely unnecessary, because using `np.trapz()` is easy, more accurate, and doesn't require any assumptions. Replace Eq. 15-18 with
```
f_x = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)  # a range of x's
tan_psi_top_fx = _sky_angle_tangent(gcr, tilt, f_x)  # a range of angles corresponding to x
psi_top_x = np.arctan(tan_psi_top_fx)  # a range of angles
y = (1 + np.cos(psi_top_x + tilt)) / (1 + np.cos(tilt)
f_sky_pv_shade = np.trapz(y, x)
```

Unfortunatly /all works in an other way you would expect.
Let's say we have a Tuple with `[e1,e2]` - now your expectation is to select this Tuple by using this expression:
`Tuple/all(e: e eq 'e1' or e eq 'e2')`
But this expression will only return these Tuples:
`[e1,e1] or [e2,e2]`
because /all means every element in the list have to meet the condition.
With the announced feature for a future tm1 version could you select the Tuple with this expression:
`(Tuple/1 eq 'e1') and (Tuple/2 eq 'e2') `
With this feature we're able to refactor the function in tm1py ;-)

The original code handled multiple channels
```python
def _split_and_resample_wav(origAudio, start_time, stop_time, new_wav_file):
    nChannels = origAudio.getnchannels()
    sampleWidth = origAudio.getsampwidth()
    frameRate = origAudio.getframerate()
    origAudio.setpos(int(start_time * frameRate))
    chunkData = origAudio.readframes(int((stop_time - start_time) * frameRate))
    # by doubling the frame-rate we effectively go from 8 kHz to 16 kHz
    chunkData, _ = audioop.ratecv(chunkData, sampleWidth, nChannels, frameRate, 2 * frameRate, None)
    chunkAudio = wave.open(new_wav_file, "w")
    chunkAudio.setnchannels(nChannels)
    chunkAudio.setsampwidth(sampleWidth)
    chunkAudio.setframerate(2 * frameRate)
    chunkAudio.writeframes(chunkData)
chunkAudio.close()
```

- `spinel_encoder.hpp` is included here which in turn includes `spinel.h`.

- In OpenThread `SPINEL_PLATFORM_HEADER` is mapped to [`spinel_platform.h`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/ncp/spinel_platform.h#L31) which `#include "utils/wrap_string.h"`

Not sure changes like this are worth it, but if you must, please put them in their own commit.

> For some of our file patterns we need more than just the filename. Some readers encode the file type structure into directories so we really need a majority of the path.

That makes it of course a lot more complicated and makes it hard to use duck types which are not aware of a directory structure, e.g. file objects.
At the same time, I am wondering about this design choice. I do not know the details, and I guess there is a good reason for it. However, I have seen long discussions about filenames, and even if I do not see the same value in these names as others, I agree that it is a decent possibility to easily identify products, but it appears strange to me that a reader also constrains the user on how he/she needs to arrange his/her directory structure.
I guess the design choice traces back to the combination of file identifier and file location, but here an example could help to improve my understanding.
I also feel like I have not fully understood the function of the pattern. Is the pattern only used as a filter to determine which file can be read by the given reader? Or is the pattern also used to find appropriate files on the file system? Or is the pattern also used for the mapping from filename to reader (if yes, what is the tiebreaker if a filename matches the pattern of multiple readers?)?

Still, I do not see a problem with duck types. If we implement the duck inspection and let the readers, that put more constraints on their ducks, raise exceptions in case of missing attributes, it would not break any workflow.

> [...] any idea if there is a pathlib feature I'm missing here [...]

A `pathlib.Path` will not be a problem, because it is very aware of the directory structure. Beside the `.name` attribute, it also provides the `.parent` for the directory, `.parents` for direct access to other levels in the directory hierarchy, and `.parts` to split the path into directory names...

> I would have all filenames parsed with urllib [...]

If it is a string, we can parse it to get an object providing our desired attributes (where I personally prefer `yarl` over `urllib`), otherwise, we just pass whatever object was given to the reader and expect/hope that our users know what they are doing.

I guess that most reader patterns only constrain the base name of a given file path, so in most cases the `.name` attribute would be sufficient for a duck. If more is needed, the `.path` or `.parts` attribute could be the next thing to look for.

> I think we cannot totally make no assumptions when parsing LinkMetricsQueryOptions. Actually I think kMaxTypeIdFlags is an assumption about it. Regarding future extension for other types, we can make kMaxTypeIdFlags a little larger.

Let me describe the scenario where we will face compatibility issues and challenges
- Suppose we use the PR as is and put a hard limit in max typid in the message or in the way the (sub)TLVs are parsed (order/number of sub-TLVs in a message, etc).
- Then say product A is shipped running this implementation.
- It will work fine while all devices are running the same version of sw/OT stack and follow same format.
- Now suppose in next version of thread spec more type ids are defined and/or new sub-TLVs are added.
- We update the OT stack and add support for the new types id and new features.
- Now a product B is shipped running the new version of OT stack.
- We expect product A still running earlier stack version to continue to work with product B running new version (which can now add new sub-TLVs and/or type ids, etc. when sending its requests).
- If we do not implement the parsing/processing of message in a flexible way, we will create a challenge requiring sw updates or patches for all in-field devices for features to continue to work.

-------

> What about reading all TypeIdFlags into a buffer once and pass the buffer to AppendSingleProbeLinkMetricsReport to filter the ones we know?

What buffer size would we use to be safe and compatible all future extensions here? The challenge is that we cannot safely know the size. What we do know currently is which (and how many) type-ids we support in the current implementation. Let me describe my suggestion from above with more details:
- We define a buffer `typeIdFlags[kMaxTypeIdFlags]` with `kMaxTypeIdFlags` (`kMaxTypeIdFlags` is number of type ids we know how to handle and support in current implementation)
- We read the type ids from the TLV and message one by one and if it is one of the ones we know, we add it in `typeIdFlags[]`, if not we just ignore it
    - Basically we read and filter the array of type ids from the message into a subset of type-ids which we know how to handle.
    - If we see duplicate type-id entries, we can consider it invalid (give parse error) or may choose to ignore it (not sure if spec defined any specific behavior - if not specified then I guess it's up to us/developer to decide a reasonable behavior).
- We then pass `typeIdFlags[]` (and its length) to `AppendSingleProbeLinkMetricsReport()`.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils._pytree import tree_flatten, tree_unflatten


class Transform(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()

        return dict()

    def _transform(self, input: Any, params: Dict[str, Any]) -> Any:

        # pass-through types

        flat_outputs = [

Here is a simple example of card entanglement via a shared state reference
1) The user expands the card.
`card1State = {tableExpanded: true}`
The card state map is now `{ card1: card1State}`
2) The user pins the card.
The card state map is now `{card1: card1State, pinnedCard1: card1State}`
3) The user collapses the pinned cards table
The card state map "unchanged"  `{card1: card1State, pinnedCard1: card1State}`
Except that `card1State = {tableExpanded: false}` thus collapsing the table for both cards.

I believe that this _is_ the correct behaviour. For once, the docs are clear and explicit:

> "A ConjunctiveGraph is an (unnamed) aggregation of all the named graphs in a store.
> 
> It has a ``default`` graph, whose name is associated with the graph throughout its life.
> :meth:`__init__` can take an identifier to use as the name of this default graph or it
> will assign a BNode."

`graph_id` is asserted to be the identifier of the ConjunctiveGraph and therefore the
identifier of the default graph and, as the quad uses `graph_id` as the context identifier,
the statement appears in the default graph, as described in the docstring and the
serialization is correct.

The quad after parsing is:
```py
[
    (
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/subject'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/predicate'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/object'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/graph')
    )
]
```

AIUI, the isomorphic difference comes from the _parsing_ of that (IMO) correct serialization, the result of which is:
```py
[
    (
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/subject'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/predicate'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/object'),
        rdflib.term.BNode('N8b0826bb0fcb40cc88aeafe1a6964898')
    )
]
```
which in turn is serialized as:
```n3
@prefix ns1: <http://example.com/> .

_:N8b0826bb0fcb40cc88aeafe1a6964898 {
    ns1:subject ns1:predicate ns1:object .
}
```
it starts getting complicated at that point because if a quad with a `BNode` context identifier is added:
```py
    quad2 = (EG["SUBJECT"], EG["predicate"], EG["object"], rdflib.BNode())
    graph.add(quad2)
```
the quads become:
```py
[
    (
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/SUBJECT'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/predicate'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/object'),
        rdflib.term.BNode('n3b8c44770ac14158bb50244d3caf978ab1')
    ),
    (
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/subject'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/predicate'),
        rdflib.term.URIRef('http://example.com/object'),
        rdflib.term.BNode('N12d002dd5a6e459a955ffb38d326dcf9')
    )
]
```
and the graph is trig-serialized as:
```trig
@prefix ns1: <http://example.com/> .

_:n3b8c44770ac14158bb50244d3caf978ab1 {
    ns1:SUBJECT ns1:predicate ns1:object .
}

_:N12d002dd5a6e459a955ffb38d326dcf9 {
    ns1:subject ns1:predicate ns1:object .
}
```
whilst (AFAICT) the RDFLib trig parser can successfully round-trip this serialization, I don't believe the semantics are exportable.

Moving the timer to `Transport` might indeed make sense! The reason for having both the check in the queue and the timer thread is that for cases with low load (especially when testing locally), you might not have enough requests to trigger the flush without an outside trigger. We used to not have the timer trigger, and got "agent doesn't send data" support requests all the time, which turned out to be cases where the user created a transaction and then waited for the transaction to appear, without making further requests to their service. Having this external trigger avoids that problem.

This goes back to [3.8.1](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.schema/commit/6369d354c8d318bcda224d758243b6d31b598199). Previously it was setting a `_v_` attribute on each object as it was validated, but that lead to security issues, apparently.

As for a leak, the code is careful to push and pop object ids in a finally block, so the actual values shouldn't be leaking. If you're referring to the thread-local objects themselves leaking as threads are created and deleted, I don't think that's a problem. I believe this because I once [broke that for gevent and got complaints](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/981). Under Python 2.7, local objects are stored in a Thread `__dict__` and so [automatically go away when the thread does](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/_threading_local.py), and under 3.4 they are explicitly handled with [weakrefs](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.4/Lib/_threading_local.py).

As for the scope, name, and visibility, this is used from a function, so it either needs to be module scoped or added as a hidden kwarg default to the function. I can do that, or just stick an `_` in front of it.

Also, since we're looking at it, I would like to change it to not use the object's `__dict__` directly. On PyPy, at least, those are optimized for `str` keys, and we're just shoving ints in there (PyPy may adjust that strategy, but I think it would have to do so anew for each thread.). Using an explicit attribute (a `set`) of the thread-local object would be more clear, IMO.

since this is the same assertion code above, you can probably just collapse them into the same test case, e.g.:

```python
def test_unrelated_logger_loaded_last_in_debug(run_python_code_in_subprocess, ddtrace_run_python_code_in_subprocess):
    env = os.environ.copy()
    env["DD_TRACE_DEBUG"] = "true"
    code = """
import ddtrace
import logging
custom_logger = logging.getLogger('custom')
custom_logger.setLevel(logging.WARN)
assert custom_logger.parent.name == 'root'
assert custom_logger.level == logging.WARN
"""

    out, err, status, pid = run_python_code_in_subprocess(code, env=env)
    assert status == 0, err
    assert err != b""
    assert out == b""

    out, err, status, pid = ddtrace_run_python_code_in_subprocess(code, env=env)
    assert status == 0, err
    assert err != b""
    assert out == b""
```

paired with the parametrize means you can get a ton of coverage from a single test.

it does pack a lot of different assertions (with + without ddtrace-run) into a single test case, but given the setup code and multi-line string as code, it might help maintainability in the long run.

```go
	var wroteDiagnosticHeader = false

The variable now acts as an in/out flag that allows the value to be passed in, but also allows the callee to update it with their updated value.

Please don't remove the check on `mean_squared_error`. Such checks are an important way to detect if a proposed future change might impact statistical performance (see, for example, this discussion: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4228#issuecomment-862710227).

Since `mean_squared_error` has a scale relative to the scale of the target, I think it makes sense that you'd get such different values for two different datasets.

I just ran `lgb.train()` using the same parameters as this test, but:

* on latest commit to `master` (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/commit/4aaeb22932b72aaff632b40ba596ca2533071ca2)
* using `boosting_type = "goss"`

<details><summary>sample code (click me)</summary>

```python
import lightgbm as lgb
import numpy as np
from sklearn.datasets import make_regression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = make_regression(n_samples=10_000, n_features=10, n_informative=5)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)
lgb_train = lgb.Dataset(X_train, y_train)
lgb_eval = lgb.Dataset(X_test, y_test, reference=lgb_train)
params1 = {
    'boosting_type': 'goss',
    'metric': 'l2',
    'verbose': -1
}
evals_result1 = {}
gbm = lgb.train(params1, lgb_train,
                num_boost_round=10,
                valid_sets=lgb_eval,
                callbacks=[lgb.record_evaluation(evals_result1)])

# compute the MSE if the model just predicted the mean of the target from training data
print(np.mean(np.square(y_test - np.mean(y))))

#  check evaluation results on validation data
print(evals_result1)
```

</details>

I observed that for this dataset + set  of parameters, the mean squared error from just using the mean of the target in the training data to predict on the test data is `26348.54`.

The mean squared error on the test data from the final iteration of LightGBM training is `7320.57`, a noticeable improvement.

So I'd recommend a test like, say, `mean_squared_error(y_test, gbm.predict(X_test)) < 7500`. I chose a slightly higher value than the observed validation loss to avoid this test becoming flaky (i.e. failing due to minor variations in performance caused by randomness in the training process).

this happened due to the removal of `T extends Resource ? { } : ...` that came in: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/commit/ad4da29cbddee7d9ddfb59f638cb3375e6c15b07#diff-b7a9c3c1793336a377df01cb5e0e9f40

Previously, the `any` cases in `Output<any>` would be caught by this, effectively adding no members to `Output<any>`.   i.e. `Output<any> = OutputInstance<any> & {}` TS seems to have refined things slightly so that this now becomes `Output<any> = OutputInstance<any> & Lifted<{}>` which it feels is no longer compatible because objects are indexable.  

My theory is that this fell out of TS 3.7's [recursive type aliases](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-3-7.html#more-recursive-type-aliases) work (something which we could benefit from).  Basically, instead of aggressively flattening the type, they lazily do it, but it means that they can see different types here which now triggers some subtle assignability issue.

Regardless, the above should be strictly better and works downlevel and on 3.7.  It does really indicate what we want, namely that only 'objects/arrays' should have their properties lifted up, and anything else should not participate in lifting.

I’d be open to it, since the dead-read-store won’t cost much, but it
gets weird once you add preemptions:

```rust
if self.capacity == 0 {
    return;
}
self.preempt(step);
self.seen += 1;
```

It’s important to preempt before incrementing `seen`. And it seems good
to return on zero-capacity before preempting—otherwise, `preempt` has to
be more careful to work in the zero-capacity case.

Since `seen` is not exposed and reservoir capacity can’t be changed
after construction, this should be unobservable—and I was careful to
test the full public API in `test_empty` for this reason. So I think
I’ll keep it as is, though I don’t feel super strongly about it.

@simonlingoogle, that's always the main goal and and  in many cases when investigating why some test fail rarely, we find some issue/bug in the some part of the the code that we fix (in some cases it's an issue in core OT, or sometime in the platform layer simulation or driver/wpantund). 
In some cases it can be assumption in the way the test script is written which we address (a good example of this case is #4516).

That said, the retries are for issues/failures that are usually beyond our immediate control and  may be related to OS/processes/sockets/dbus/pipes, etc. For example, socket tx (which we use for posix simulation) is not guaranteed to be 100% reliable, or IP messages can be dropped/delayed by netif (used by wpantund), or port number being blocked (test cannot bind to randomly selected port number). Many of these are rare but possible (e.g., perfectly valid for linux `netif` to drop an IPv6 message). 

Note that some of the tests run with very high speed-up generating a lot of events/actions that can overflow system/processes (e.g., the `test-602-channel-manager-channel-select.py` emulates about 12 hours of network operation within 4.5s, the test can be made more robust by running it at slower speed up  but at the end, it made more sense to run it fast (to help make the average test run time shorter) and allow retry for occasional failures.

> `find_program (<VAR> name ...)`

> A cache entry named by <VAR> is created to store the result of this command. If the program is found the result is stored in the variable and the search will not be repeated unless the variable is cleared. If nothing is found, the result will be <VAR>-NOTFOUND, and the search will be attempted again the next time find_program is invoked with the same variable.

> Is there any reason why we cannot do this from the platform layer code and need it to be emitted from OT core?

Yes, we can implement the status emitting to `OTNS` in platform code, after following enhancements to OT core:
* Add missing callbacks that is used by `OTNS`
* Enhance each callback to support multiple callbacks
* Enhance Child/Neighbor callbacks to be able to pass the corresponding `ExtAddress`

However, with such effort and increased code complexity, what we get is a strict binding of `OTNS` to the simulation platform, which makes it difficult to port `OTNS` to other platforms. 

Meanwhile, the current implementation has all logic code ready in OT core and requires only one platform function `otPlatOtnsStatus` to be implemented. For example, we can implement `otPlatOtnsStatus` for `NRF52840 Dongle` to emit the status information through UART to visualize devices that connected to the host computer. 

Overall, I think using callbacks in platform code does not have strong benefits, but impose restriction to future `OTNS` enhancements. 

---
Currently, `OtnsStatus` calls are scattered in OT core, which I think should be refactored.  **It would be better if we can use one core component just for `OTNS` related code.** However there is currently no mechanism for signalling synchronous events among core components.

Okay I thought about this and I want `check_invariants` to be purely non-mutating, and so it should only check invariants that have already been parsed/added, and *not* call `_set_invariants` here. So please remove this call, but you can keep the helper function refactoring you made.

This also has an implication for the test you added, since you'll need to add the `@check_contracts` decorator to your class. This means that you can't violate the representation invariant when you initially create the object (since an `AssertionError` will be raised), but instead will need to create a valid object then mutate it to violate an RI. I suspect doing something like `self.age -= ...` will work, but if not, you can create an attribute that's a mutable object and then *mutate* that attribute value's object to violate an RI, e.g. `self.something.append(...)`. PythonTA doesn't check RIs after one of its attribute objects is mutated.

The problem is that the `requests` library decodes the response based on the content-type that the HTTP response header defines (in the `charset` field). The Mollie API doesn't specify a charset, so `requests` falls back to the default which is ISO-8859-1. Decoding the JSON with ISO-8859-1 will fail if there are unicode characters.

The RFC that originally defined the ISO-8859-1 default 
(see https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-3.7.1), is superseded by other RFCs, and the new default is "The default charset of ISO-8859-1 for text media types has been
   removed; the default is now whatever the media type definition says." (see https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#appendix-B).

As JSON is defined to always be in unicode, and in a perfect world, `requests` would be able to detect this based on the `Content-Type: application/hal+json` header, but it doesn't. Technically this might not even apply, since the RFC only removes the default in text media types; JSON is an application media type.

In a different perfect world, Mollie would send a `Content-Type: application/hal+json charset=utf-8` header, which is recognized by `requests` and removes confusion about which unicode charset would be used. We communicated about this with @ndijkstra and @willemstuursma, unfortunately without any response from their side.

Since Mollie defines their JSON responses to be in UTF-8, we decided to hardcode the value here based on the media type. Feel free to try and fix the Mollie server responses, and we'd be happy to remove the hardcoded value.

@djmitche I just want your confirmation that removing this code makes sense to you as well.
`taskcluster-treeherder` in this case emits two Pulse messages: one for the current task + one for the previous *run* which was retried.

The message for the previous run (notice `message.runId-1`) would be ingested by Treeherder and would update the previous run as `retry`:

```python
async def handleTaskRerun(pushInfo, task, message):
    let job = this.buildMessage(pushInfo, task, message.runId-1, message);
    job = buildMessage(pushInfo, task, message["runId"]-1, message)
    job["state"] = "completed"
    job["result"] = "fail"
    job["isRetried"] = True   # <-- This is what makes Treeherder mark a job as `retried`
```

Now, all of these comments refer to this diff specifically (a compare with all related commits):
https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/pull/5042/files/a23e935467dc7667ce7e76bc75a5c1338b6aa246..566d61bf334a44a6e9f24dd7eba3afd45b1b875e

We can know if an excepted task should be considered as retried by checking the  "reasonResolved":
https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/pull/5042/files/a23e935467dc7667ce7e76bc75a5c1338b6aa246..566d61bf334a44a6e9f24dd7eba3afd45b1b875e#diff-609378e93352a69d9c31bfe55b0a8d64R254

    # Based on https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/blob/1a224a54c17fe7871c39b56e185268082623760f/services/queue/schemas/v1/task-exception-request.yml
    # and https://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/blob/f6ed2f3819e4cbaabc8c422852113803b103058f/services/queue/schemas/v1/task-status.yml#L81
    if jobRun["reasonResolved"] in ("worker-shutdown",
                                    "intermittent-task",
                                    "claim-expired"):
        job["isRetried"] = True
```

On a theoretical level, yes, and I would argue that for site specific analysis then uncertainty on the site property itself this should be treated as an epistemic uncertainty. Though for a site specific analysis you would not be following this approach anyway. On a practical level, it is inevitable that for regional scale risk analysis you will have (sometimes considerable) uncertainties on the site parameter, but it would be extremely difficult to treat these epistemically in the way that OpenQuake (or any other software) currently does. So unless you accept a higher sigma in the ground motion variability it is highly likely that this uncertainty will just get dropped out of the hazard and risk model altogether, and I would argue that is worse than treating epistemic as aleatory for the time being. If you have been running large scale hazard and risk calculations using Vs30 from Wald & Allen but applying a GMPE calibrated whose site term is calibrated on a measured Vs30, then so far this site parameter uncertainty has been omitted (or otherwise substantially underestimated) from the analysis.

```python
@unittest.skipIf(simulator_check(lambda simclass: not simclass.supports_vhpi())
```

So the 1e-6 isn't really giving me the correct timing anyway.  Eventually, I'll look into seeing if there is another method that I can use to get closer to the spec sheets for the displays but for now, slower still works.

As far as correct placement in the code, each parallel device has its own requirements but 1e-5 is likely to be above the required value for pretty much any display.  So, leaving it in luma.core.interface.serial makes sense.

good callout -- dug more into the profiling, and there's two things going on. first, in the original code, we were calling `croniter.match` anyway, so removing extra calls to get_prev/get_next will always result in a speed improvement. second (perhaps more importantly), the time taken to get the previous tick is correlated to the distance between those ticks. Inside of `match`, they're calculating the previous tick of the schedule, relative to 1 microsecond after the input time (so the previous tick will only be one microsecond away. Inside the other codepath, we're getting the true previous tick, which is, in my example, 15 minutes away. You can see the difference in time spent in this profile: The left hand side is the time spent on "get_prev" in the old codepath, whereas the right hand side is the time spent on get_prev in the "match" codepath. If you dig in even further, the fast codepath calls `proc_second` inside of `_calc`, whereas the slow codepath calls `proc_minute`.

I'll note that replacing `apply_annotations` with this keeps all tests passing, reduces the size of the "fork" coming from `if metadata_get_schema is not None`, and eliminates the need for the interior binding of the metadata/metadata_get_schema that is only necessary due to being in the for loop:
```
def apply_annotations(
    get_inner_schema: ModifyCoreSchemaWrapHandler,
    annotations: typing.Iterable[Any],
    definitions: dict[str, core_schema.CoreSchema],
) -> ModifyCoreSchemaWrapHandler:
    """
    Apply arguments from `Annotated` or from `FieldInfo` to a schema.
    """
    for metadata in annotations:
        if metadata is None:
            continue
        get_inner_schema = get_wrapped_inner_schema(get_inner_schema, metadata, definitions)

    return get_inner_schema


def get_wrapped_inner_schema(
    get_inner_schema: ModifyCoreSchemaWrapHandler, metadata: Any, definitions: dict[str, core_schema.CoreSchema]
) -> Callable[[Any, ModifyCoreSchemaWrapHandler], core_schema.CoreSchema]:
    metadata_get_schema = getattr(metadata, '__get_pydantic_core_schema__', None) or (
        lambda source, handler: handler(source)
    )

    def _new_inner_schema_handler(
        source: Any, handler: ModifyCoreSchemaWrapHandler = get_inner_schema
    ) -> core_schema.CoreSchema:
        schema = metadata_get_schema(source, handler)
        schema = apply_single_annotation(schema, metadata, definitions)
        metadata_js_modify_function = _get_pydantic_modify_json_schema(metadata)
        CoreMetadataHandler(schema).compose_js_modify_functions(metadata_js_modify_function)
        return schema

    return _new_inner_schema_handler
```

```python

AWS_SES_FROM_EMAIL = "my_default_from_email@email.com"

            "from": "ignored_from_email@email.com",

> If docker throws: (ImageNotFound),  something else that extends NotFound, this may cause unexpected issues. 

Good point.

> I think where we are leaning is to handle the failure but not enable skip_pull_image and let customers define this through the command line.

I feel that querying the registry on every request is over the top to avoid running a "very" outdated version which is why these solutions are setting the `skip_pull_image` flag. In my workflow (so naturally everyone else's too ;-)) I'd only risk running an outdated version for maybe a day. I don't know that I have ever left a development server running for a week or more. 

With that said, would removing the skip toggle from the code and suggesting the user re-run the daemon with `--skip-pull-image` to stop it from pulling an image on every refresh suffice?.  So something like this: 

```python
except DockerNotFound as e:
    # See Also: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17726
    LOG.warning(
        "Cannot check if base image is current because an unknown 404 error was returned. "
        "This might be due to an incompatible Docker engine clone. Consider rerunning "
        "with the --skip-pull-image argument. Pulling image."
    )
    LOG.debug("Error unknown 404 response from Docker Engine", exc_info=e)
```

Without the flag a broken docker daemon would pull the image on every request slightly irritating the user with slow response times and battery drain. 

Including the --skip-pull-image suggestion would of course run the risk of the user unintentionally including that flag as they hit up in the shell to rerun the command resulting in a very old image being used so I'm also open to replacing the suggestion for the flag with a suggestion that "If this problem persists try reinstalling the official Docker engine" or something similar.

> I think at the very least, we need to check what kind of status code it is...

From the traceback in the bug, what we're getting is `docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("b'Not Found'")`. I think the 404 status code will be the same for an ImageNotFound from the registry and the base NotFound we're getting from the daemon so there's not a good way to differentiate between the two types of errors. `is_client_error` will return `True` for both without some upstream work.

An alternative to checking status code would be to check that the exception caught by `except DockerNotFound` is actually that class instead of a subclass. All together it'd look something like this:

```python
except DockerNotFound as e:
    if not hasattr(e, "__class__") or e.__class__ is not DockerNotFound:
        # This is most likely a subclass of the base exception we're trying to catch
        # so raise the error that way it can be handled.
        raise
        
    # Otherwise we aren't able to check for new images so pull a fresh one to be safe.
    # See Also: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17726
    LOG.warning(
        "Cannot check if base image is current because an unknown 404 error was returned. "
        "This might be due to an incompatible Docker engine clone. Consider rerunning "
        "with the --skip-pull-image argument. Pulling image."
    )

There's a lot of duplication of metadata throughout. Not crucial, but you could simplify the maintenance of your module using CLIArgumentTypes. Here's a pattern used in Network.

```Python
account_name_type = CLIArgumentType(options_list=['--account-name', '-a'], id_part='name', help='Name of the ANF account.')

with self.argument_context('netappfiles') as c:
  c.argument('account_name', account_name_type)

with self.argument_context('netappfiles account') as c:
  c.argument('account_name', account_name_type, options_list=['--account-name', '-a', '-n', '--name'])

with self.argument_context('netappfiles pool list') as c:
  c.argument('account_name', account_name_type, id_part=None)
```

This will allow you to change the help text for account name in only one place instead of several, for example.

@CristiFati That would be because the site-packages aren't yet updated at the point when `pywin32_bootstrap.py` is being executed.

Giving some more details, looking here: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/site.py#L494-L503
That `addsitepackages(known_paths, [sys.prefix])` invocation will trigger the `.pth` file load/execution down the line, which will in turn call `pywin32_bootstrap.py`.
But `site.getsitepackages()` is based on the `PREFIXES` global variable, which is updated afterwads.

My goal was not to check that the columns are treated as categorical features by LightGBM. My goal was to ensure that having pandas "category" columns in your training data does not break LightGBM's Dask estimators (as it currently does on `master`, documented in #3861).

As far as I can tell, "category" columns will only be  automatically treated as categorical features if you set  `categorical_feature = 'auto'`: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/8ef874bd4804b54b48ef4d796c8110be671a6950/python-package/lightgbm/basic.py#L513-L514, [which is not the default](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Parameters.html#categorical_feature), and of course you can force it to happen by setting `categorical_featue` to the column names / indices in the parameters.

The tests like `assert_eq(p1_local, p2)` already test that the Dask interface is producing a model that is similar to the one produced by the scikit-learn interface.

I can add `categorical_feature = [col for col in dX.columns if col.startswith('cat_')]` and test, that's fine. I will try to do that tonight. But it wasn't the intention of this PR or these tests.

The `wheel` package allows to "execute" the wheel whl file itself. Sounds strange, but it's true. :wink: To support that, our semver `__main__.py` needs to be adapted like this:

```python
import sys

def main():  # needed for console script
    if __package__ == '':
        import os.path
        path = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(__file__))
        sys.path[0:0] = [path]
    import semver.cli
    sys.exit(semver.cli.main())


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main())
```

With that change, it's possible to "run" the wheel like this:

```
$ python3 semver-3.0.0*.whl/semver -h
usage: semver [-h] [--version] {compare,bump,check,nextver} ...

[... help output pruned ...]
```

```align_corners=False/True``` are two different mode of bilinear interpolation or some other interpolation algorithms.
Before this pr:
1. The CPU code of ```nd.Resize``` op use opencv's bilinear interpolation, which is equivalent to BilinearResize2D with argument ```align_corners=False```. 
2. The GPU code of ```nd.Resize``` op use BilinearResize2D internally, but BilinearResize2D implicitly use ```align_corners=True```.
Therefore, I've added the ```align_corners``` argument and add ```align_corners=False``` mode to BilinearResize2D. 
After py*ch 0.4.0, ```align_corners=False``` is the default mode of its interpolate op. Deeplab v3 claims that use ```align_corners=True``` can improve segmentation accuracy. As I've tested, ```align_corners=False``` is more efficient.

I've confirmed that the following workaround makes the linter happy, but is quite hacky:

Add `type_hint_rename_types` to `RenameSchemaTypesVisitor` as another class attribute (parallels the [`rename_types`](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/schema_transformation/rename_schema.py#L179) frozenset that already exists)

```
type_hint_rename_types = Union[
    EnumTypeDefinitionNode,
    InterfaceTypeDefinitionNode,
    NamedTypeNode,
    ObjectTypeDefinitionNode,
    UnionTypeDefinitionNode,
]
```

Then we can use this `Union` as the type in the function signature for `_rename_name_and_add_to_record` and replace the call [`self._rename_name_and_add_to_record(node)`](https://github.com/kensho-technologies/graphql-compiler/blob/master/graphql_compiler/schema_transformation/rename_schema.py#L268) with 

```
renamed_node = self._rename_name_and_add_to_record(
    cast(RenameSchemaTypesVisitor.type_hint_rename_types, node)
)
```

Trying to turn the existing set to a mypy `Union` doesn't work syntactically (e.g. iterating over these 5 types don't work) as far as I can tell, but I'd be very happy to be proven wrong!

Due to the existence of non customized commands (such as `delete` and `list-operations`) and their operation path is hard coded as `azure.mgmt.msi.operations#xxx`. 
If `ResourceType.MGMT_MSI: '2018-11-30'` configuration is not added to the old profiles, CLI will report an error when it executes a non customized command in the old profile which without adding ``ResourceType.MGMT_MSI`.
![Screenshot 2022-03-30 211508](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16612065/160845984-2fcc30f1-57ab-4c34-91f0-ad05820298d2.png)

This is because when loading arguments, a imports the operation path as hard coded path, which will cause the package from operation path cannot be found because the SDK package is a multiple version package.
code link: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/45842acafb076d58f66511c46192e039e3f815f0/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/command_operation.py#L41-L59
![Screenshot 2022-03-30 211424](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16612065/160846269-49426dae-e0bc-4c10-9e93-64db21a741ad.png)

When we add the configuration `ResourceType.MGMT_MSI` in the profile,  the import operation path is the correct path containing the version number in the multiple version SKD
![Screenshot 2022-03-30 210707](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16612065/160846514-75a8d3dd-41ad-4ce3-9b62-142c2a6e7a9a.png)

In audio, to reduce the boilerplate code for individual testing, we took an approach similar to 2. We break tests into cases based on kinds of test and dtype/devices. In https://github.com/pytorch/audio/tree/main/test/torchaudio_unittest/transforms, we have autograd tests, batch consistency tests, third party consistency tests, torch script consistency tests, and transform-specific correctness tests. > shouldn't the project.path in the ManifestProject be identical to the config manifest.path

Yes, but we are trying to get rid of ManifestProject (#327), so I am trying to slowly phase out any in-tree users.

Let me explain my long term goals for after this PR. They are:

#. add a new `west.manifest.Manifest.__init__` kwarg: `legacy_manifest_projects: Optional[bool] = None`. If `legacy_manifest_projects` is `True`, then `Manifest.projects` will include the `ManifestProject` at index 0. If it is `False`, then `Manifest.projects` will **not** include the `ManifestProject`. For compatibility, if `legacy_manifest_projects` is None, then issue a warning about changing behavior and then work the same as if it were `False`.
#. manually set `legacy_manifest_projects` to `True` in static methods in the Manifest class (i.e. `west.manifest.Manifest.from_topdir()`, `west.manifest.Manifest.from_file()`, and `west.manifest.Manifest.from_data()`), so users see no differences and get no warnings (since people in practice use these methods instead of calling `Manifest.__init__` directly
#. Add new **module-level** functions to `west.manifest`: `from_topdir()`, `from_file()`, and `from_data()`, which set `legacy_manifest_projects` to `False`. (I'm talking about a new `west.manifest.from_topdir()` function here, **not** `west.manifest.Manifest.from_topdir()`, etc.)
#. Move all in-tree users over to the new module-level functions, including this part of project.py. Add special case helpers that retain backwards compatibility so the manifest repository is still considered in `west list` output and any other places where it's currently needed (details TBD)
#. Deprecate the static methods (`west.manifest.Manifest.from_topdir()`, etc.)
#. Eventually, remove both the static methods and `ManifestProject`. Some details TBD; I haven't thought it all the way through yet as I'm just trying to fix this one bug in this PR.

At that point, `west list` will not see a `ManifestProject`, and it will need to be using `self.manifest.repo_abspath` and other related fields to access path information for the manifest itself as is being done in the current re-work. I'm laying the ground for that transition to be a bit easier later on in this change by making `west list` get things from the right place for long-term use.

But I take your point that calling self.config is a little ugly here, so I'll add a new `Manifest.repo_path` attribute which caches the configuration option.

That said, it is often advised to version-pin external actions, but the purpose of that is to avoid unexpected breaking changes. I usually do not worry too much about that because a failure to run either of these steps due to breaking changes will be caught immediately. This is more like failing tests due to upstream changes, which is often a good thing because one can keep up with upstream changes incrementally in a timely manner. Version/commit-pinning means we end up using potentially insecure code in the long run if we are not vigilant about keeping dependencies up to date and incorporate all the security patches. On the contrary, I would go for version pinning up to the minor version (in semver style) when it comes to application dependencies to prevent any surprises when the application is run (not when tested).

Now for CUDA Graphs to function, we rely on the fact that the tensor's gpu dptr will remain unchanged, but what about  the TBlob instance?  ShapeComputeGPU() is receiving a reference to the inputs and is not copying the TBlob, but can the op rely on the fact that the TBlob is not copied at some higher level of graph handling?  A copy of the input TBlob instance would preserve the dptr, but would change the cpu-memory pointer value returned by in_data.shape_.data().

So bottom line, I think this might work to have the Op declared 'CUDA Graphs compatible', but it relies on a different 'contract' with the rest of the software.

Is there a strong reason why ID generation cannot live outside of python-arango (e.g. in your application code or in some shared utils package)?

Bulk generating keys from python-arango's side (without proper support from ArangoDB server for atomic operations) doesn't seem like a good idea due to key collision on concurrent requests or retry after failure, unless it's something like uuid4.

Supporting incremental and deterministic key generation for example, means python-arango would have to keep track of ID(s) internally. I think python-arango should aim to be as stateless as possible.

One solution here would be to allow users to pass in a `KeyGenerator` object on collection creation with method like `MyCustomKeyGenerator.generate_next_key()`. This way we would be placing the burden of about managing state and concurrency on the user.

In serve pipeline `build` we would carry new info from user such as variable name, for a DAG like

with ServeInputNode(preprocessor=request_to_data_int) as dag_input:

with vcr.use_cassette('httpx_and_requests.yaml'):

Using that, I get [this output generated](https://gist.github.com/the-allanc/fcb9ea8c260050211cb198b198b1798a) - for the JSON resource, both httpx and requests generate a regular string (without the `!!binary` marker). For the GZIP resource, requests creates a binary string, but httpx does not.

The only reason that the gzip resource differs between requests and httpx is because requests seems to allow you to get the raw data stream (which hasn't been decoded - i.e. un-gzipped), whereas I had trouble accomplishing that with httpx - so we store the decoded (un-gzipped) text and remove the Content-Encoding response header.

I think it's fine. We can support the following actually:

1. return any kind of normal workflow in virtual actor method
2. inside workflow virtual actor method, call another method and pass a complicated workflow to that method
```
@workflow.virtual_actor
class Actor:
    # what I mean by 1)
    def method(self, param):
         return complicated_workflow.step()

   # what I mean by 2)
   def method2(self):
        start = [complicated_workflow.step(), complicated_workflow_2.step()]
        all = join.step(*start)
       # any kind of workflow is ok
        self.update.step(all)
   
    def update(self, msg):
       # msg is the result for complicated_workflow

    # what can't do
    # self.method.step is not returned directly
    # instead, it returns a workflow, and inside it it's the virtual actor method call (workflow)
    def self_sub_workflow(self):
        all = join.step([self.method.step(), self.method2.step()])
        return all
```

By "return the numpy read array", I meant do this:

```
read_array[:]=response.read_array
return read_array, response.samps_per_chan_read
```

You did that, but you also changed the code to allocate a temporary numpy array:
```
read_array[:] = numpy.array(response.read_array)
return read_array, response.samps_per_chan_read
```

The reason why some of the read functions accept a numpy array from the user is to avoid unnecessarily allocating and copying arrays. DAQmx applications may work with large amounts of data, so when working on DAQmx read/write functions, we should assume that allocations and copies are expensive.

The right hand side of `read_array[:] = ...` does not need to be a numpy array. It can be a list:
```
>>> import numpy
>>> read_array = numpy.zeros(3, dtype=numpy.double)
>>> read_array
array([0., 0., 0.])
>>> read_array[:] = [1.1,2.2,3.3]
>>> read_array
array([1.1, 2.2, 3.3])
```

Or a tuple:
```
>>> read_array[:] = (3.3,4.4,5.5)
>>> read_array
array([3.3, 4.4, 5.5])
```

Or a Protobuf repeated field:
```
>>> import nidaqmx._stubs.nidaqmx_pb2
>>> response = nidaqmx._stubs.nidaqmx_pb2.ReadAnalogF64Response()
>>> response.read_array.extend([6.6,7.7,8.8])
>>> response
read_array: 6.6
read_array: 7.7
read_array: 8.8

>>> read_array[:] = response.read_array
>>> read_array
array([6.6, 7.7, 8.8])
```

from typing import Hashable


class col:
    """Helper class for column selection within an expression.

    This class is used to specify a column to be used in an expression, such as a comparison or arithmetic operation.

    Args:
        column (Hashable): The name of the column to be selected.

    Raises:
        TypeError: If the `column` parameter is not hashable.

    !!! info "New in version 0.25.0"

    !!! warning

        `col` is currently considered experimental.
        The implementation and parts of the API
        may change without warning.

    """

    def __init__(self, column: Hashable):
        """Initialize a new instance of the `col` class.

        Args:
            column (Hashable): The name of the column to be selected.

        Raises:
            TypeError: If the `column` parameter is not hashable.
        """
        self.cols = column
        check("column", self.cols, [Hashable])
        self.join_args = None

    def __gt__(self, other):
        """Implement the greater-than comparison operator (`>`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, ">")
        return self

    def __ge__(self, other):
        """Implement the greater-than-or-equal-to comparison operator (`>=`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, ">=")
        return self

    def __lt__(self, other):
        """Implement the less-than comparison operator (`<`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, "<")
        return self

    def __le__(self, other):
        """Implement the less-than-or-equal-to comparison operator (`<=`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, "<=")
        return self

    def __ne__(self, other):
        """Implement the not-equal-to comparison operator (`!=`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, "!=")
        return self

    def __eq__(self, other):
        """Implement the equal-to comparison operator (`==`).

        Args:
            other (col): The other `col` object to compare to.

        Returns:
            col: The current `col` object.
        """
        self.join_args = (self.cols, other.cols, "==")
        return self
```

Hmm. I still have significant concerns about showing cumulative averaging across all training steps - I'd like to discuss further in a follow-up. Not resetting at epoch boundaries (and using a non-decaying average) means that any initial outlier values at the start of training will bias the rest of the curve indefinitely, which could be very misleading. For example, see this screenshot:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/710113/192877103-ef65d1f3-5c34-4461-bd8c-8898570bca2d.png)

By the end of 5 epochs, the `batch_level` instantaneous loss has pretty much plateaued around 0.1, but the `batch_avg` is still up at 0.2, even though the epoch-level average loss has been below 0.2 since the second epoch (as shown in the logging that `model.fit()` produces or the built-in Keras summaries that write epoch-level loss values).  If all the user wants is smoothing of the batch-level variation, TensorBoard's smoothing algorithm can provide that with greater accuracy, and in a way that can always be adjusted or disabled later to see the true data.

I'll approve to get this PR unblocked, but if we keep this behavior, I'd prefer that you at least reinstate some kind of warning or note in the text, to ensure users realize that this method of averaging will equally weight loss from all batches seen during training, and hence won't match the built-in per-epoch loss metric.

If a task function wants to set extra metadata, it can do that in its validator by doing `task_func.extra_metadata={"some": "thing"}`. This `extra_metadata` is binded to the registered job instance's `extra_metadata`, which gets passed to the job constructor via `_ready_job`. 

Every job that is registered will have its own `RegisteredJob.extra_metadata` so there's no chance of metadata being shared wrongly between multiple jobs.

Oh I see. Reading through the code it looks like the logic is a bit more subtle than just ignoring values that begin with `_` though. It looks like it ignores values that are "sunder" or "dunder" only (i.e. `_amount_` or `__amount__`). For example if I construct the following Enum:

```python
In [4]: enum = Enum('foo', [('amount', 'Amount'), ('_amount', 'Amount'), ('__amount', 'Amount'), ('__amount__', 'Amount')])

# It looks like this:
In [5]: enum._meta.enum.__members__
Out[5]:
mappingproxy({'amount': <foo.amount: 'Amount'>,
              '_amount': <foo.amount: 'Amount'>,
              '__amount': <foo.amount: 'Amount'>})
```

(If I try and construct an enum with `('_amount_', 'Amount')` it just fails completely)

From a GraphQL spec point of view names that begin with `__` are not allowed because they are reserved for introspection but names that begin with `_` are allowed. So based on that I would recommend that the logic here be something like:

```python
if _is_sunder(name) or _is_dunder(name):
	name = "A%s" % name
if name.startswith('__'):
	name = "A%s" % name
```

You should strongly consider using an interceptor to specify metadata.

Reasons:
- An interceptor would automatically add metadata for all calls.
- You wouldn't have to modify any call sites, which would make this a lower-touch change. Instead, you would instantiate an interceptor object, call `grpc.intercept_channel(channel, interceptor)`, and use the channel that it returns.

We have been using interceptors to add metadata in C#. It's really easy with that API because it has a metadata interceptor built in and it provides an overload of `ChannelBase.Intercept()` that takes a `Func<Metadata, Metadata>`. I don't know if the gRPC Python API provides a built-in metadata interceptor, but writing a metadata interceptor for unary-unary calls should not be complicated.

> We can use port number 53 but it may add some inconveniences in the servers side implementation...

@abtink I don't think so. I think we will be violating the [IANA registration rules](https://www.iana.org/assignments/service-names-port-numbers/service-names-port-numbers.xhtml) if we use `53` for SRP server. As we know, the SRP protocol is a subset of the [RFC2136 DNS UPDATE](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2136) (which uses port `53`) with additional constrains on the DNS records. A server which provides both standard DNS UPDATE and SRP service will not be able to tell whether a DNS message is a SRP message or just a normal DNS UPDATE message.

I considered this however it adds a lot of complexity and some (but minimal) performance overhead (~4-8ns) when trace_id is accessed. I tried adding the following properties `Span._trace_id64lower` and `Span._trace_id64upper`. I converted Span.trace_id in to a derived property. When `trace_id` was accessed I used bitwise operations to combine `_trace_id64lower` and `_trace_id64upper` into one integer. However in this design `_trace_id64upper` was never used directly. The more performant approach was to generate one 128bit trace_id and convert it into hex directly (higher order bits are never sent as an integer). 

Another approach is to cache all three ids. But in this scenario if `trace_id`, `_trace_id64upper`, or `_trace_id64lower` is updated we will need to ensure all the values in sync. The additional complexity does not appear to be worth it. 

All in all, `Span.trace_id` is accessed in many places but `Span._trace_id64lower` is only accessed when a trace is encoded and `Span._trace_id64upper` will never be used directly. I think the current approach has the best performance and is the easiest to maintain. Thoughts?

In the update mode, the parameter value is not obtained from mc because we just need to use the value provided by the user to overwrite the value in mc.

But in the create mode, mc is completely blank, and we want to assemble the user's input into a complete mc. Considering that our parameters have a certain dependency or mutual exclusion relationship, or the parameters will be normalized before putting into mc, there are even some parameters that do not need user to provide explicitly, but can be dynamically completed by cli.

In order to ensure the consistency of the state and make the value of the parameter more clearly determined, we created the following principle in the create mode, that is, if a parameter has been placed in the mc object, then when trying to get the parameter value again, (e.g. cross-validation with other parameters) the value in mc shall prevail.
